verses.text: The LORD said unto Joshua: Fear not, neither be thou dismayed: take all the people of war with thee, arise, go up to Ai: I have given into thy hand the king of Ai, his people, his city, his land. Thou shalt do to Ai and her king as thou didst unto Jericho and her king: only the spoil and the cattle shall ye take for a prey unto yourselves. Lay thee an ambush for the city behind it. Guidance: God's first word after Achor is 'Fear not.' Discipline does not estrange Him; it cleanses the relationship. This time He grants Israel the spoil, the very thing Achan grasped at — held lawfully it is gift; stolen it was death. And God Himself recommends a tactic: the same God who toppled walls also designs ambushes. Faith does not exclude planning; it just keeps planning under orders.

The LORD said unto Joshua: Fear not, neither be thou dismayed: take all the people of war with thee, arise, go up to Ai: I have given into thy hand the king of Ai, his people, his city, his land. Thou shalt do to Ai and her king as thou didst unto Jericho and her king: only the spoil and the cattle shall ye take for a prey unto yourselves. Lay thee an ambush for the city behind it. Guidance: God's first word after Achor is 'Fear not.' Discipline does not estrange Him; it cleanses the relationship. This time He grants Israel the spoil, the very thing Achan grasped at — held lawfully it is gift; stolen it was death. And God Himself recommends a tactic: the same God who toppled walls also designs ambushes. Faith does not exclude planning; it just keeps planning under orders.

Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up against Ai: chose thirty thousand mighty men of valour, sent them away by night. Commanded them: Behold, ye shall lie in wait against the city behind it: go not very far from the city, but be ye all ready. I and all the people with me will approach the city: when they come out against us, as at the first, we will flee before them; they will come after us till we have drawn them from the city, for they will say, They flee before us, as at the first; therefore we will flee. Then ye shall rise up from the ambush, seize the city: the LORD your God will deliver it into your hand. When ye have taken the city, ye shall set the city on fire: according to the commandment of the LORD shall ye do. See, I have commanded you. Joshua sent them; they went to lie in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of Ai: Joshua lodged that night among the people. Guidance: The earlier defeat is repurposed. The men of Ai will assume Israel runs again because they already saw Israel run once. Old failure becomes new strategy when faith handles it. Joshua spends the night with the troops, not in his command tent — leaders who have just been broken stay close to those who broke with them.

Joshua rose up early in the morning, numbered the people, went up, he and the elders of Israel, before the people to Ai. All the people of war went up, drew nigh, came before the city, pitched on the north side of Ai: there was a valley between them and Ai. He took about five thousand men, set them to lie in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city. When they had set the people on the north and their liers in wait on the west, Joshua went that night into the midst of the valley. Guidance: A second ambush layer — 5,000 alongside the 30,000. Joshua does not minimize the previous defeat by under-committing; he over-prepares. And he walks into the valley alone at night before the battle. Where leaders sleep tells you where they trust.

When the king of Ai saw it, they hasted and rose up early, and the men of the city went out against Israel to battle, he and all his people, at a time appointed, before the plain; but he wist not that there were liers in ambush against him behind the city. Joshua and all Israel made as if they were beaten before them, fled by the way of the wilderness. All the people in Ai were called to pursue: they pursued after Joshua, and were drawn away from the city. There was not a man left in Ai or Bethel that went not out after Israel: they left the city open, pursued after Israel. Guidance: The king reads chapter 7 and assumes it is also chapter 8. Pride blinds him to the new variable: the LORD is back among Israel. Memory of past advantage often becomes the trap for the next round. The city gates are left open — the most fortified place in Ai is now its emptiest. Sin makes us leave our doors open without knowing it.

The LORD said unto Joshua: Stretch out the spear that is in thy hand toward Ai; for I will give it into thine hand. Joshua stretched out the spear toward the city. The ambush arose quickly out of their place, ran as soon as he had stretched out his hand: entered into the city, took it, hasted, set the city on fire. Guidance: Joshua's outstretched spear echoes Moses' outstretched staff at the Red Sea and Amalek. The gesture is the signal, but it also is intercession — held up, like the rod that won the battle while it stayed up (Exod 17). The ambush charges at the visible sign of God's leader committed to the fight.

The ambush arose quickly out of their place, ran as soon as he had stretched out his hand: entered into the city, took it, hasted, set the city on fire. Guidance: Joshua's outstretched spear echoes Moses' outstretched staff at the Red Sea and Amalek. The gesture is the signal, but it also is intercession — held up, like the rod that won the battle while it stayed up (Exod 17). The ambush charges at the visible sign of God's leader committed to the fight.

When the men of Ai looked behind them, they saw, and, behold, the smoke of the city ascended up to heaven, they had no power to flee this way or that way: the people that fled to the wilderness turned back upon the pursuers. When Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city, that the smoke ascended, then they turned again, slew the men of Ai. The other issued out of the city against them; so they were in the midst of Israel, some on this side, some on that side: they smote them, so that they let none remain or escape. The king of Ai they took alive, brought him to Joshua. Guidance: The men of Ai turn and see their home on fire — and lose all 'power to flee this way or that.' Sin caught between memory and consequence has nowhere to run. The trap closes from both sides. The king, who was first into the fight, is the only one taken alive — for a slower judgment that will declare what hangs over every defiance of God.

When Israel had made an end of slaying all the inhabitants of Ai in the field, in the wilderness wherein they chased them, when they were all fallen on the edge of the sword, until they were consumed, all the Israelites returned unto Ai, smote it with the edge of the sword. All that fell that day, men and women, were twelve thousand, even all the men of Ai. For Joshua drew not his hand back, wherewith he stretched out the spear, until he had utterly destroyed all the inhabitants of Ai. Guidance: Joshua's spear stays raised all day. The narrator notes it deliberately — as Moses' arms had to be held up by Aaron and Hur until sunset, so Joshua holds his own spear up until the work is finished. Leaders cannot drop the sign while their people are still in the fight. Persistence in the lifted hand is part of the victory.

Only the cattle and the spoil of that city Israel took for a prey unto themselves, according unto the word of the LORD which he commanded Joshua. Joshua burnt Ai, made it an heap for ever, even a desolation unto this day. The king of Ai he hanged on a tree until eventide: as soon as the sun was down, Joshua commanded that they should take his carcass down from the tree, cast it at the entering of the gate of the city, raise thereon a great heap of stones, that remaineth unto this day. Guidance: Spoil is taken — the test Achan failed. Same opportunity, different ending, because permission has been given. The king is hanged until evening, then taken down before sunset (Deut 21:23) — even the law of the curse-tree is honored. A heap of stones at the city gate becomes the third stone-pile of the book: covenant memorial, Achan's grave, and now Ai's king. Israel keeps building cairns to remember.

Then Joshua built an altar unto the LORD God of Israel in mount Ebal, as Moses commanded the children of Israel, as it is written in the book of the law of Moses, an altar of whole stones, over which no man hath lift up any iron: they offered thereon burnt offerings unto the LORD, sacrificed peace offerings. He wrote there upon the stones a copy of the law of Moses, which he wrote in the presence of the children of Israel. Guidance: Right after victory, Joshua builds an altar — not a triumphal arch. Unhewn stones, no chisel, because human craft does not improve God's gift (Deut 27:5-6). The first writing project in the land is the law itself, inscribed on plastered stones so anyone passing can read it. Conquest does not begin a new code; it ratifies the old one.

All Israel, and their elders, and officers, and their judges, stood on this side the ark and on that side before the priests the Levites, which bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, as well the stranger as he that was born among them; half of them over against mount Gerizim, half of them over against mount Ebal; as Moses had commanded before, that they should bless the people of Israel. Afterward he read all the words of the law, the blessings and cursings, according to all that is written in the book of the law. There was not a word of all that Moses commanded which Joshua read not before all the congregation of Israel, with the women, and the little ones, and the strangers that were conversant among them. Guidance: The covenant assembly Moses scripted in Deut 27 — exactly performed, two generations later. Half the nation echoes blessings from Gerizim; half echoes curses from Ebal. The phrase 'with the women, the little ones, and the strangers' is the chapter's most tender note: the law is read in full, no edit, no abridgment, with every age group present. Children hear the curses; immigrants hear the blessings. God's word reaches the whole household, not the head of it.
